text.title: spec-writing Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for spec-writing. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: A complete spec must contain: Overview, Problem Statement, Goals (with current/target metric table), Non-Goals, User Stories, Functional Requirements (FR-N IDs), Non-Functional Requirements, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Open Questions. Missing any section is a reject signal.
rule.rationale: Each section prevents a specific class of delivery failure: Non-Goals prevent scope creep, Open Questions prevent false confidence, Acceptance Criteria prevent untestable scope.

rule.statement: Every goal must have a measurable current baseline and a numeric target. "Improve UX" is not a goal. "Reduce time-to-find-product from 3 min to 30 sec" is a goal.
rule.rationale: Unmeasurable goals cannot be verified at launch and cannot drive prioritization decisions during delivery.

rule.statement: List everything explicitly excluded from this spec. Non-Goals section must be non-empty for any feature touching more than one surface.
rule.rationale: The absence of Non-Goals is the leading cause of scope creep. Every unspecified behaviour becomes "obviously included" during implementation.

rule.statement: Every story uses the format: "As a [named persona], I want to [action] so that [measurable benefit]." Use persona IDs (not "the user") in every story. Include the "so that" clause—it encodes motivation and validates the story is worth building.
rule.rationale: Stories without persona IDs cannot be traced to a validated user segment. Stories without "so that" cannot be evaluated for user value.

rule.statement: Functional requirements use "FR-N: The system shall [do X]" format. Cap at ~15 FRs per spec; larger features must be split. Keep implementation details out: no library names, DB schemas, or UI component names.
rule.rationale: Specs mixing what and how box in engineers and are ignored. Implementation decisions belong in the design document, linked from the spec.

rule.statement: Open Questions section must be non-empty. A spec with zero open questions signals false confidence. List technical unknowns, pending business decisions, and user research gaps with an owner per question.
rule.rationale: LLMs in particular will "answer" every question rather than acknowledge uncertainty. Explicitly requiring a non-empty Open Questions section forces intellectual honesty.

rule.statement: Target 1-3 pages (or ~15 FRs). Use Mini-Spec for features under one sprint. Track decisions made post-approval as a versioned addendum—never silently rewrite the original spec.
rule.rationale: Beyond 5 pages, nobody reads the spec. Silent rewrites destroy the audit trail that later resolves scope disputes.

rule.statement: Assign a unique ID to every FR (FR-1, FR-2, ...) and reference that ID in the corresponding acceptance criteria. An FR without a linked AC is an orphan—reject the spec.
rule.rationale: Without ID-based traceability, FRs and ACs drift apart during revisions. Orphan FRs produce untested scope at launch.

rule.statement: Label every FR with a priority: Must (MVP blocker), Should (high value, deferrable), Could (nice-to-have). Pair with a co-signer for any FR labelled Must.
rule.rationale: Unprioritized requirements produce all-or-nothing scope debates at sprint planning. Must-level FRs need sign-off because they encode strategic decisions.

rule.statement: Every NFR must specify a measurable criterion. "The system shall be fast" is not an NFR. "FR-2 latency: results visible within 200ms at p95 for up to 50K concurrent users" is an NFR.
rule.rationale: Unmeasurable NFRs cannot be verified in QA and become perpetual technical debt claims.

rule.statement: Every FR must have at least one Given/When/Then acceptance criterion. Each AC row must reference the FR ID. AC must be unique—never a restatement of the FR.
rule.rationale: AC that restates the FR cannot be used for test case authoring or sprint review verification. "FR-2 search works → AC: search works" tells testers nothing.
